annotations.label: Chaos theory

anchor.snippet: Chaos theory is an interdisciplinary area of scientific study and branch of mathematics

annotations.note: Mathlib has no definition of chaos theory or chaotic systems as such.

annotations.label: Butterfly effect

anchor.snippet: The butterfly effect , an underlying principle of chaos, describes how a small change in one state of a deterministic nonlinear system can result in large differences in a later state

annotations.note: The butterfly effect / sensitive dependence is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Deterministic chaos

anchor.snippet: This behavior is known as deterministic chaos , or simply chaos .

annotations.note: No notion of deterministic chaos exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lorenz's summary of chaos

anchor.snippet: When the present determines the future but the approximate present does not approximately determine the future

annotations.note: This is an informal characterization with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Lyapunov time

anchor.snippet: a time scale depending on the dynamics of the system, called the Lyapunov time

annotations.note: Grep for 'Lyapunov' returned no dynamical-systems results in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Exponential growth of forecast uncertainty

anchor.snippet: doubling the forecast time more than squares the proportional uncertainty in the forecast

annotations.note: No formalization of forecast-uncertainty growth in chaotic systems.

annotations.label: Devaney definition of chaos

anchor.snippet: to classify a dynamical system as chaotic, it must have these properties

annotations.note: Devaney's three-condition definition of chaos is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Last two properties imply sensitivity

anchor.snippet: the last two properties above have been shown to actually imply sensitivity to initial conditions

annotations.note: The Banks et al. theorem (transitivity + dense periodic points imply sensitivity) is absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Continuous maps on metric spaces

anchor.snippet: In the discrete-time case, this is true for all continuous maps on metric spaces

annotations.note: Not formalized, since sensitivity itself is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: On intervals transitivity implies the rest

anchor.snippet: If attention is restricted to intervals , the second property implies the other two

annotations.note: The Vellekoop–Berglund result for interval maps is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Weaker definition of chaos

anchor.snippet: An alternative and a generally weaker definition of chaos uses only the first two properties

annotations.note: No definition of chaos (weak or strong) exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sensitivity to initial conditions

anchor.snippet: Sensitivity to initial conditions means that each point in a chaotic system is arbitrarily closely approximated by other points

annotations.note: Sensitive dependence on initial conditions is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lorenz's definition of the butterfly effect

anchor.snippet: Lorenz defined the butterfly effect as

annotations.note: Lorenz's formulation of the butterfly effect is not formalized.

annotations.label: Lyapunov exponent

anchor.snippet: the Lyapunov exponent measures the sensitivity to initial conditions, in the form of rate of exponential divergence from the perturbed initial conditions

annotations.note: No definition of Lyapunov exponents was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Positive MLE indicates chaos

anchor.snippet: A positive MLE, coupled with the solution's boundedness, is usually taken as an indication that the system is chaotic

annotations.note: The maximal-Lyapunov-exponent chaos criterion is not formalized.

annotations.label: Periodic sequences are repelling

anchor.snippet: such periodic sequences are repelling rather than attracting

annotations.note: No formalization of repelling vs. attracting periodic orbits in this sense.

annotations.label: Topological mixing

anchor.snippet: Topological mixing (or the weaker condition of topological transitivity) means that the system evolves over time so that any given region or open set of its phase space eventually overlaps with any other given region

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the weaker condition of topological transitivity but not topological mixing itself.

annotations.label: Doubling map: sensitivity without mixing

anchor.snippet: consider the simple dynamical system produced by repeatedly doubling an initial value

annotations.note: The doubling map y ↦ n•y on the circle appears in Mathlib (proven ergodic), but the illustrative sensitivity-without-mixing claim is not formalized.

annotations.label: Topological transitivity

anchor.snippet: is said to be topologically transitive if for any pair of non-empty open sets

annotations.note: Mathlib defines topological transitivity for monoid actions, generalizing the single-map open-set-overlap condition.

annotations.label: Birkhoff Transitivity Theorem

anchor.snippet: The Birkhoff Transitivity Theorem states that if X is a second countable

annotations.note: Mathlib has transitivity/density characterizations but not the Birkhoff theorem equating transitivity with existence of a dense orbit in second-countable spaces.

annotations.label: Dense orbit implies transitivity

anchor.snippet: the existence of a dense orbit implies topological transitivity

annotations.note: Mathlib proves minimality (all orbits dense) implies transitivity, but not the weaker hypothesis of a single dense orbit.

annotations.label: Density of periodic orbits

anchor.snippet: For a chaotic system to have dense periodic orbits means that every point in the space is approached arbitrarily closely by periodic orbits

annotations.note: Mathlib defines the set of periodic points but has no named 'dense periodic orbits' predicate.

annotations.label: Logistic map period-2 orbit

anchor.snippet: is one of the simplest systems with density of periodic orbits

annotations.note: The logistic map and its periodic orbits are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Li–Yorke: period three implies chaos

anchor.snippet: any continuous one-dimensional system that exhibits a regular cycle of period three will also display regular cycles of every other length

annotations.note: Neither the Li–Yorke nor Sharkovskii period-three theorem is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Chaotic attractor

anchor.snippet: The cases of most interest arise when the chaotic behavior takes place on an attractor

annotations.note: No definition of attractor was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Strange attractors

anchor.snippet: the attractors that arise from chaotic systems, known as strange attractors , have great detail and complexity

annotations.note: Strange attractors are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Julia set

anchor.snippet: a repelling structure called a Julia set , which forms at the boundary between basins of attraction of fixed points

annotations.note: Grep found no Julia set definition in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lorenz attractor

anchor.snippet: This attractor results from a simple three-dimensional model of the Lorenz weather system

annotations.note: The Lorenz attractor is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Coexisting chaotic and non-chaotic attractors

anchor.snippet: coexisting chaotic and non-chaotic may appear within the same model (e.g., the double pendulum system)

annotations.note: Coexisting attractors and the double pendulum are not formalized.

annotations.label: Finite-dimensional linear systems never chaotic

anchor.section: Minimum complexity of a chaotic system
anchor.snippet: Finite-dimensional linear systems are never chaotic; for a dynamical system to display chaotic behavior, it must be either nonlinear or infinite-dimensional

annotations.note: This result presupposes a chaos definition that Mathlib lacks.

annotations.label: Poincaré–Bendixson theorem

anchor.section: Minimum complexity of a chaotic system
anchor.snippet: the Poincaré–Bendixson theorem shows that a strange attractor can only arise in three or more dimensions

annotations.note: The Poincaré–Bendixson theorem is not formalized in Mathlib (only an unrelated Poincaré conjecture file exists).

annotations.label: Zhang–Heidel: minimal quadratic systems

anchor.section: Minimum complexity of a chaotic system
anchor.snippet: three-dimensional quadratic systems with only three or four terms on the right-hand side cannot exhibit chaotic behavior

annotations.note: The Zhang–Heidel non-chaos result for minimal quadratic systems is not formalized.

annotations.label: Lorenz system equations

anchor.section: Minimum complexity of a chaotic system
anchor.snippet: The Lorenz attractor discussed below is generated by a system of three differential equations

annotations.note: The Lorenz system of ODEs is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sprott minimal chaotic system

anchor.section: Minimum complexity of a chaotic system
anchor.snippet: found a three-dimensional system with just five terms

annotations.note: Sprott's minimal chaotic system is not formalized.

annotations.label: Non-Euclidean 2D chaos

anchor.section: Minimum complexity of a chaotic system
anchor.snippet: two-dimensional continuous systems with non-Euclidean geometry can still exhibit some chaotic properties

annotations.note: Chaos on non-Euclidean 2D systems is not formalized.

annotations.label: Chaos in infinite-dimensional linear systems

anchor.snippet: chaos may occur also in linear systems, provided they are infinite dimensional

annotations.note: Linear chaos in infinite dimensions (e.g. hypercyclic operators) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Infinite-dimensional coupled maps

anchor.snippet: The straightforward generalization of coupled discrete maps

annotations.note: Coupled map lattices are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Kuramoto synchronization conditions

anchor.snippet: In the Kuramoto model , four conditions suffice to produce synchronization in a chaotic system

annotations.note: The Kuramoto model and its synchronization conditions are not formalized.

annotations.label: Combinatorial chaos

anchor.snippet: such as combinatorial chaos (I.e. applying recursively a discrete combinatorial action)

annotations.note: No notion of combinatorial chaos exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Poincaré's nonperiodic orbits

anchor.snippet: Henri Poincaré found that there can be orbits that are nonperiodic, and yet not forever increasing nor approaching a fixed point

annotations.note: Poincaré's result on bounded nonperiodic orbits in the three-body problem is not formalized.

annotations.label: Hadamard's billiards instability

anchor.snippet: Hadamard was able to show that all trajectories are unstable

annotations.note: Hadamard's instability result for geodesic flow on negatively curved surfaces is not formalized.

annotations.label: Fractal (self-similarity)

anchor.snippet: An object whose irregularity is constant over different scales

annotations.note: Mathlib defines Hausdorff dimension (the mathematical notion of fractal dimension) but has no definition of a fractal or self-similar set.

annotations.label: Koch curve / snowflake

anchor.snippet: which is infinitely long yet encloses a finite space and has a fractal dimension of circa 1.2619

annotations.note: Mathlib's Snowflaking construction is motivated by the von Koch snowflake in its docstring, but the Koch curve and its fractal dimension are not actually constructed or computed.

annotations.label: For want of a nail folklore analogy

anchor.section: A popular but inaccurate analogy for chaos
anchor.snippet: The sensitive dependence on initial conditions (i.e., butterfly effect) has been illustrated using the following folklore

annotations.note: This is a folklore illustration with no mathematical content to formalize.
